project_goal.rationale: Core clinical workflow replacement for legacy PACS viewer

target_users.value: Radiologists and radiology technologists at a large hospital network
target_users.rationale: Primary end users identified in stakeholder workshops

risk_level.rationale: PHI in DICOM metadata; clinical decision support tool with patient safety implications

data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for metadata and reports; Redis for worklist session caching

project.goal: Replace legacy PACS viewer with Electron desktop app for DICOM study reading and reporting

frontend.state: Redux Toolkit for worklist and viewport state

backend.api: Internal REST API for worklist and report endpoints

reasoner_reviews.product: Worklist-to-report flow is the critical path. Prior study comparison is a top radiologist request that should not slip to Phase 3. The structured reporting module needs configurable templates per modality to meet subspecialty radiology requirements.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Electron with WebGL DICOM rendering is sound for thick-client performance. The FastAPI backend should be lightweight — heavy DICOM pixel processing happens in the Electron renderer process using a WASM-compiled DICOM toolkit. PACS C-MOVE integration needs a DICOM SCP service running locally or as a network daemon.
reasoner_reviews.security: PHI embedded in DICOM tags (patient name, DOB, MRN in 0010,xxxx tag group) requires metadata stripping before any cloud transmission. AAD MFA enforcement must cover the Electron app OAuth PKCE flow, not just web browser sessions.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: <2s study load on LAN is achievable for CT series up to 500 slices with pre-fetch caching. MRI studies with 2,000+ frames may exceed this without background pre-loading. Redis-based prefetch scheduler needed.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 3 plan had thin observability — no DICOM-specific metrics like study load time histograms or worklist queue depth. The deployment topology section did not address how the DICOM SCP daemon is managed alongside the Electron app.

backend.service_design: FastAPI microservices: worklist-service (C-FIND queries), report-service (structured report CRUD), annotation-service (geometry storage), auth-service (AAD token validation)
backend.api_patterns: REST with OpenAPI 3.1; DICOM web (WADO-RS) for image retrieval alongside DIMSE for PACS compatibility
backend.failure_handling: Circuit breaker on PACS C-MOVE calls; retry with exponential backoff; graceful degradation to cached worklist on PACS unavailability
backend.scaling_notes: Stateless FastAPI pods behind Azure Load Balancer; Redis cluster for shared session state across multiple workstation instances

frontend.app_structure: Electron main process manages DICOM SCP daemon and IPC; renderer process hosts React SPA with WebGL viewport
frontend.pages_and_flows: Worklist View, Study Viewer (multi-viewport layout), Report Editor, Annotation Toolbar, Prior Study Comparison Panel
frontend.state_management: Redux Toolkit slices for worklist, active study, viewport layout, and report draft state
frontend.performance_notes: DICOM frame prefetching in Web Worker threads; WASM-compiled DICOM decoder for zero-copy pixel processing; progressive loading prioritizes first 50 frames

security.auth_design: MSAL.js PKCE flow in Electron renderer; access tokens stored in OS keychain via keytar, never in localStorage
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every DICOM study open, annotation save, and report finalization logged with user ID, patient MRN hash, timestamp, and workstation ID
security.privacy_controls: DICOM tag scrubbing enforced before any study data leaves hospital network perimeter

data.entities: imaging_studies (study_uid, patient_id, modality, study_date, status, assigned_radiologist), dicom_series (series_uid, study_uid, modality, frame_count, storage_path), radiology_reports (id, study_uid, radiologist_id, report_text, status, finalized_at), annotations (id, series_uid, frame_number, geometry_json, annotation_type, created_by), worklists (id, radiologist_id, filters_json, last_refreshed)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL for report and annotation metadata; Azure Files NFS share for DICOM pixel data; Redis for active worklist cache
data.retention_and_deletion: DICOM studies archived to Azure Blob cold tier after 2 years; reports retained 7 years in PostgreSQL with logical deletion only

devops.deployment_topology: Azure VM fleet per radiology department; DICOM SCP daemon as Windows Service; FastAPI services on AKS sidecar; Azure Files mounted as NFS
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint, pytest, Electron build, code-signing, MSI packaging, staged deploy to dev then staging VMs
devops.observability_stack: Azure Monitor for VM metrics; Grafana for DICOM-specific dashboards (study load time p50/p95, worklist queue depth, C-MOVE failure rate)
devops.rollback_strategy: MSI versioned installers allow silent rollback to previous build via SCCM push

target_output.title: Hospital Radiology DICOM Workstation Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: An Electron desktop application for hospital radiologists to read and report DICOM studies (CT, MRI, X-ray) with FastAPI backend services on Azure VMs integrated with the existing PACS via DIMSE. Key capabilities include WebGL multi-viewport rendering, worklist management, structured reporting, and prior study comparison.
target_output.architecture_overview: Thick-client architecture with an Electron renderer process hosting a React 18 SPA with WebGL DICOM viewports communicating via IPC to the main process, which manages a pynetdicom DICOM SCP Windows Service for C-FIND/C-MOVE operations. FastAPI backend services run on Azure VMs behind Azure Load Balancer, providing REST APIs for worklists, reports, and annotations. DICOM pixel data is stored on Azure Files NFS share; study metadata and reports persist in PostgreSQL 16. Azure Active Directory provides SSO and MFA via MSAL PKCE flow. All PHI access events are forwarded to Azure Monitor.
target_output.technology_stack: Desktop: Electron 30, React 18, TypeScript, Redux Toolkit, WebGL DICOM renderer, WASM decoder. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, Pydantic v2, pynetdicom for DIMSE. Database: PostgreSQL 16 for metadata and reports, Redis 7 for worklist session cache. Storage: Azure Files NFS for DICOM pixel data, Azure Blob cold tier for archival. Auth: Azure Active Directory, MSAL.js with PKCE. Infra: Azure Virtual Machines, Azure Load Balancer, GitHub Actions CI, SCCM for workstation deployment.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Worklist Engine displaying assigned and unread studies filtered by modality, body part, and priority. Multi-viewport DICOM Viewer with windowing, zoom, pan, and measurement tools per modality. Structured Report Editor with configurable templates per CT/MRI/X-ray subspecialty. Annotation Module storing geometry overlays (length, area, angle) per DICOM frame. Prior Study Comparison fetching matched historical series side-by-side. Analytics Dashboard for radiology productivity metrics.
target_output.system_components: Electron Main Process managing DICOM SCP Windows Service via child_process and IPC bridge. React Renderer hosting viewport grid and report editor. pynetdicom DICOM SCP daemon (Windows Service, auto-restart, SCCM-managed). FastAPI Worklist Service querying PACS via C-FIND. FastAPI Report Service for CRUD on radiology_reports. PostgreSQL 16 primary with daily Azure Backup. Redis 7 for worklist prefetch cache. Azure Files NFS mount for DICOM pixel data.
target_output.workflows: Study Read Workflow: Radiologist opens worklist, selects a pending CT study; Electron triggers C-MOVE to retrieve series from PACS to Azure Files; Web Worker decodes DICOM frames in WASM; WebGL viewport renders first 50 frames within 1.8s on hospital LAN; radiologist annotates and finalizes report; FastAPI saves report and updates study status to read. Priors Matching Workflow: On study open, backend queries patient MRN against PostgreSQL to retrieve prior study UIDs; matched series are background-fetched via C-MOVE and pre-loaded into Redis frame cache; radiologist activates comparison layout showing current and prior side-by-side without manual search.
target_output.data_model: imaging_studies (study_uid VARCHAR PK, patient_mrn_hash, modality ENUM ct/mri/xray/us, study_date, status ENUM unread/in_progress/read, assigned_radiologist_id, pacs_ae_title). dicom_series (series_uid PK, study_uid FK, frame_count INT, storage_path_azure_files, series_description). radiology_reports (id UUID PK, study_uid FK, radiologist_id, template_id, report_text, status ENUM draft/finalized, finalized_at). annotations (id UUID PK, series_uid FK, frame_number, geometry_json JSONB, annotation_type ENUM length/area/angle/roi). worklists (id UUID PK, radiologist_id, filter_config_json JSONB, last_refreshed_at).
target_output.api_design: Internal REST API at /api/v1. GET /worklist?modality=CT&status=unread (paginated study list). POST /studies/:uid/retrieve (trigger C-MOVE from PACS). GET /studies/:uid/series (list series with frame counts). GET /reports/:study_uid (fetch draft report). POST /reports (save finalized report). POST /annotations (persist annotation geometry). GET /priors/:mrn_hash (retrieve prior study UIDs for comparison). Auth: AAD bearer token validated on every request.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HIPAA controls: AAD MFA enforced via Conditional Access policy for all radiologist and technologist accounts. MSAL PKCE token stored in OS keychain via keytar — never localStorage. DICOM tag group 0010 (patient demographics) scrubbed before any data leaves hospital perimeter. All PHI access logged (user, patient MRN hash, study UID, timestamp) to Azure Monitor Log Analytics workspace with 6-year retention. RBAC: radiologist (read/annotate/finalize), technologist (read/acknowledge), admin (configure/audit). TLS 1.3 enforced on all FastAPI endpoints. FDA 21 CFR Part 11 controls: electronic signatures on finalized reports with non-repudiation timestamps.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: DICOM SCP daemon: pynetdicom-based Windows Service installed via SCCM MSI package with auto-restart on failure (SC failure config: restart after 30s, 3 attempts then alert). Service listens on AET RADVIEW_SCP port 11112. FastAPI services containerized and deployed to Azure VMs with Azure Load Balancer. GitHub Actions CI pipeline: lint (ruff, mypy), pytest (85% coverage gate), Electron build with code-signing, MSI packaging, SCCM push to staging fleet, automated smoke test (C-FIND query, worklist API), promote to production. Rollback: SCCM silent install of previous MSI version. Environments: dev (single VM), staging (2 VMs, 5 test workstations), production (Azure VM fleet per department).
target_output.observability: Azure Monitor collects VM-level CPU, memory, and disk IOPS. Grafana dashboards with DICOM-specific panels: study load time histogram (p50/p95/p99 target <2s), C-MOVE success/failure rate per PACS AE title, worklist queue depth by modality and priority, report finalization rate per radiologist per shift. Application metrics emitted via FastAPI prometheus endpoint. Alerting: PagerDuty on C-MOVE failure rate >5% in 10-min window, study load p99 >5s, DICOM SCP Windows Service not running.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Azure VM fleet scales per department rollout phase; estimated $3,200/month at full deployment (20 VMs). Azure Files NFS storage at $0.10/GB with intelligent tiering to cold blob after 2 years. Redis Standard C2 instance sufficient for worklist cache at peak 50 concurrent radiologists.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (14 weeks): Worklist display via C-FIND, single-modality CT viewer with basic windowing, report finalization with template, DICOM SCP Windows Service, AAD authentication, CI/CD pipeline, SCCM deployment. Phase 2 (8 weeks): Multi-modality support (MRI, X-ray), annotation tools, prior study comparison with priors-matching query. Phase 3 (6 weeks): Analytics dashboard, voice dictation integration, AI detection overlay hooks, teleradiology module groundwork.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode in Electron renderer. Python type hints enforced with mypy in CI. DICOM standard compliance validated with pydicom conformance tests. 85% pytest coverage for FastAPI services. Electron security: contextIsolation enabled, nodeIntegration disabled, all IPC channels validated against allowlist. PR reviews require sign-off from one senior engineer with radiology domain knowledge.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: PACS network bandwidth saturation during peak morning reading sessions. Mitigation: C-MOVE request throttling with configurable per-radiologist concurrency limits. Risk: Electron security surface (Chromium CVEs). Mitigation: auto-update via electron-updater with signed builds. Tradeoff: WASM DICOM decoder over native module — chosen for security isolation in renderer process at cost of 15% decoding throughput. Risk: pynetdicom SCP daemon instability on Windows Server. Mitigation: Windows Service watchdog with Azure Monitor health check alerting.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected pynetdicom for DICOM SCP over commercial toolkit to reduce licensing cost. Chose Azure Files NFS over Azure Blob for DICOM storage due to POSIX file path requirements of pynetdicom. Confirmed Redux Toolkit over Zustand for complexity of multi-viewport state management. DICOM SCP daemon Windows Service deployment via SCCM confirmed with hospital IT.

metadata.notes: Round 4 radiology DICOM workstation; ISS-001 resolved in this round; plan quality weak per spec with some thin sections.

project_goal.value: Python SDK wrapping a clinical NLP pipeline to extract structured medical concepts (diagnoses, medications, dosages, procedures) from unstructured clinical notes and discharge summaries for EHR data science teams
project_goal.rationale: Core product need enabling ML teams to process clinical text at scale

target_users.value: Data science teams building ML models on EHR data inside health systems
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers identified in discovery sessions

project_class.rationale: Distributed as pip-installable package, not a hosted service

capabilities.rationale: NLP inference, concept extraction and normalization; no hosted frontend needed

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-model NLP pipeline with ontology normalization and versioned model registry

risk_level.rationale: Processes PHI in clinical notes; extraction errors could corrupt downstream ML model features

data_sensitivity.rationale: Clinical notes contain PHI including diagnoses, medications, and procedures

external_exposure.rationale: SDK consumed by internal data science teams only; no public distribution

access_model.value: Custom JWT tokens issued per data science team with project-scoped permissions for corpus and endpoint access
access_model.rationale: Prevents cross-team data access on shared SageMaker endpoints

feature_scope.value: Clinical NER for diagnoses, medications, dosages, procedures; SNOMED CT and RxNorm normalization; batch and single-document inference; model version pinning; validation corpus regression testing
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for production ML workflows

mvp_scope.value: Batch NER extraction with SNOMED CT normalization and model version pinning; no real-time streaming inference in MVP
mvp_scope.rationale: Core extraction capability needed for Q2 model training cycle

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 for all SageMaker endpoint calls; JWT authentication per team; no PHI persisted to disk or logs beyond request lifetime
security_baseline.rationale: HIPAA transmission and minimum necessary controls

frontend_stack.value: N/A - library_sdk with no UI layer
frontend_stack.rationale: SDK is pip-installable, no browser interface

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with spaCy 3.7 and Hugging Face Transformers 4.x; models served on AWS SageMaker endpoints
backend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; SageMaker provides managed GPU inference scaling

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for model version registry and annotation sets; S3 for model artifacts and validation corpora
data_platform.rationale: Relational metadata store with object storage for large model files

hosting_target.value: AWS SageMaker for model endpoints; internal PyPI registry for SDK distribution; TorchServe on bare-metal GPU for air-gapped deployments
hosting_target.rationale: SageMaker for connected environments; TorchServe for hospitals prohibiting external API calls

privacy_retention_policy.value: No PHI persisted by SDK; annotation set metadata retained 2 years per DUA; model training logs retained 90 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: HIPAA minimum necessary and institutional data use agreement requirements

future_scope.value: Real-time streaming inference for live note ingestion; relation extraction between clinical concepts; multilingual support for Spanish clinical notes

constraints.value: Must support air-gapped deployment mode for hospitals with strict network policies prohibiting external API calls; no PHI to leave hospital network perimeter
constraints.rationale: Several target health systems enforce strict data egress controls

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for SageMaker endpoint latency and per-entity-type F1 scores per model version; structured logging for extraction quality metrics
observability_baseline.rationale: ML teams need model version comparison by entity-type accuracy

execution_preference.value: Two-week sprints with monthly model release cycles
execution_preference.rationale: Decoupled cadence for code and model updates

compliance_context.value: HIPAA for any PHI processed through the SDK; institutional data use agreements (DUAs) governing training corpora; de-identification required before data leaves health system boundaries
compliance_context.rationale: Clinical text processing subject to HIPAA; DUAs restrict corpus portability across institutions

llm_integration.value: Hugging Face ClinicalBERT-family models fine-tuned on i2b2 and MIMIC-III corpora for NER; SNOMED CT lookup via QuickUMLS local index; RxNorm lookup via local SQLite snapshot (air-gapped) or RxNav REST API (connected); model artifacts stored in S3 with SHA-256 checksums
llm_integration.rationale: Domain-specific clinical NLP models outperform general LLMs for structured concept extraction from medical text

project.goal: Pip-installable Python SDK for clinical NLP concept extraction
project.target_users: Internal data science teams at health systems
project.timeline: Q2 delivery for model training cycle

backend.api: Typed dataclasses with async batch interface

security.auth: Custom JWT per team with project_scope claim
security.phi_handling: No PHI persisted beyond request scope; note content redacted from all logs

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 for model registry and annotation sets
data.storage: S3 for model artifact bundles and validation corpora

constraints.phi: No PHI to leave hospital network perimeter

reasoner_reviews.product: The SDK dual-mode design (SageMaker connected vs TorchServe air-gapped) correctly reflects heterogeneous hospital network policies. Batch-first MVP is appropriate since data science teams process clinical notes in bulk for model training, not real-time inference. SNOMED CT and RxNorm normalization are essential because raw entity text spans are insufficient for ML feature engineering across institutions using different EHR terminology.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Round 2 introduced a model version registry but the schema lacked SHA-256 checksum verification for artifacts pulled from S3. Without artifact integrity validation, a corrupted model file could silently produce incorrect extractions across an entire batch. The TorchServe air-gapped path also needs an explicit artifact bundle specification so hospital IT can perform manual transfers with predictable file structures.
reasoner_reviews.security: JWT scope validation at the SageMaker invocation boundary was not explicitly enforced in previous rounds. Per-team tokens must carry project_id claims verified against an allowlist before endpoint invocation. The SDK must not be positioned as a de-identification control -- it processes PHI and the plan must explicitly state that de-identification is an upstream responsibility.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The air-gapped constraint is architecturally significant and was insufficiently addressed in round 2. SageMaker requires internet access; the air-gapped path using LOCAL_ENDPOINT_URL must now commit to TorchServe 0.9 with a documented artifact bundle format including model.pt, tokenizer files, QuickUMLS index, and RxNorm SQLite snapshot.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Extraction quality observability remains the weakest section. Data science teams need per-entity-type precision and recall tracked per model version to compare ClinicalBERT releases. The round 2 plan referenced 'accuracy tracking' without defining metric schema or Prometheus metric names. This is ISS-003 and must be closed in this round.

backend.service_design: SDK structured as three top-level Python modules: extractor (NER pipeline orchestration via spaCy + Transformers), normalizer (SNOMED CT via QuickUMLS and RxNorm via SQLite/RxNav), registry (PostgreSQL-backed model version management with SHA-256 verification). Public API: ClinicalNLPClient(model_version, endpoint_mode). extract_batch(notes: list[str]) -> list[ExtractionResult]. extract_single(note: str) -> ExtractionResult. run_validation(corpus_id, model_version) -> ValidationReport.
backend.llm_integration_design: ClinicalBERT NER model loaded from S3 artifact bundle; SHA-256 checksum verified against model_versions.artifact_sha256 before loading. SageMakerBackend invokes endpoint via boto3 with VPC PrivateLink for connected path. TorchServeBackend calls LOCAL_ENDPOINT_URL HTTP endpoint for air-gapped path. Backend selected at ClinicalNLPClient init based on endpoint_mode param or AUTO detection via env var.
backend.failure_handling: Per-note error isolation: failed notes return ExtractionResult with error_code and error_message rather than raising. SageMaker throttling retried 3 times with 2s exponential backoff. Circuit breaker trips after 5 consecutive endpoint failures and surfaces SDK-level ClinicalNLPUnavailableError to caller. Checksum mismatch raises ModelArtifactCorruptionError and halts batch.
backend.backend_risks: Air-gapped artifact delivery depends on manual transfer; no automated update path creates risk of stale models in production. QuickUMLS SNOMED CT index freshness is a maintenance burden as SNOMED releases twice yearly -- index version tracked in model_versions table.

security.auth_design: JWT tokens issued by internal identity service carry team_id and project_scope claims. SageMakerBackend and TorchServeBackend validate token signature and scope claim before invocation. Tokens expire in 8 hours; refresh via service account credentials stored in AWS Secrets Manager.
security.privacy_controls: SDK enforces no-persistence contract: all note content processed in-memory only; no temp file writes containing PHI. Structured logs record note_id, model_version, entity_count, latency_ms -- never note text. SDK includes pre-flight pattern check for obvious PHI indicators (SSN patterns, name tokens) as a safeguard with logged warning, not a blocking de-identification control.
security.security_risks: Dependency supply chain risk: Hugging Face Transformers pulls large transitive dependency trees. All dependencies pinned in pyproject.toml with hash verification. SageMaker endpoint IAM role scoped to InvokeEndpoint only -- no model management permissions. S3 artifact bucket restricts GetObject to SDK service account role with explicit Deny for direct team access.

data.entities: model_versions (id UUID PK, name, artifact_s3_key, artifact_sha256 CHAR(64), framework_version, training_corpus, snomed_index_version, rxnorm_snapshot_date, published_at, is_active). annotation_sets (id UUID PK, team_id, corpus_name, note_count, label_schema_json, dua_reference, created_at). extracted_concept_logs (id UUID PK, job_id UUID, model_version_id FK, note_count, entity_counts_json, precision_by_type_json, recall_by_type_json, corpus_id NULLABLE FK, run_at). No clinical note content stored.
data.schema_notes: extracted_concept_logs stores aggregate quality metrics per job, never PHI. precision_by_type_json and recall_by_type_json columns store per-entity-type F1 data as JSON objects, enabling model version comparison queries across diagnosis, medication, dosage, procedure entity types.
data.data_risks: annotation_sets stores DUA references but not corpus content; corpus files remain in health-system-controlled S3. Teams may accidentally log note content in job metadata -- schema enforces VARCHAR length limits and SDK strips note content before writing job records to extracted_concept_logs.

devops.deployment_topology: Connected: SageMaker endpoint (ml.g4dn.xlarge, min 1 / max 4 instances) behind VPC PrivateLink; no public internet egress for clinical data. Air-gapped: TorchServe 0.9 on hospital-provisioned GPU server (>=16GB VRAM); model bundle transferred via encrypted SFTP within hospital network; SDK configured via LOCAL_ENDPOINT_URL env var.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions pipeline: ruff lint, mypy --strict type checking, pytest unit tests using synthetic note fixtures (zero real PHI), integration tests against SageMaker mock via moto library. Model release gate: run_validation() against labeled i2b2 corpus; fails if diagnosis_f1 < 0.95 or medication_f1 < 0.95. Passing releases published to internal PyPI with semver tag.

target_output.title: Clinical NLP Extraction SDK Architecture v3
target_output.executive_summary: A HIPAA-aware pip-installable Python SDK wrapping ClinicalBERT-family NER models to extract diagnoses, medications, dosages, and procedures from clinical notes with SNOMED CT and RxNorm normalization. Supports SageMaker inference for connected environments and TorchServe-local inference for air-gapped hospital deployments. PHI is never persisted; per-entity-type F1 metrics are tracked per model version without storing note content.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-module Python package: extractor orchestrates NER inference against either a SageMaker endpoint (boto3 via VPC PrivateLink) or a local TorchServe 0.9 instance, selected by endpoint_mode parameter or LOCAL_ENDPOINT_URL env var. normalizer maps entity spans to SNOMED CT codes via QuickUMLS local index and to RxNorm codes via local SQLite snapshot (air-gapped) or RxNav REST API (connected). registry manages model versions in PostgreSQL with SHA-256 artifact checksums verified on every load. No clinical note content passes through any persistence layer at any point.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Python 3.12 with pyproject.toml (hatch build backend). NLP: spaCy 3.7 pipeline, Hugging Face Transformers 4.x with ClinicalBERT fine-tuned on i2b2 and MIMIC-III. Inference: AWS SageMaker ml.g4dn.xlarge (connected), TorchServe 0.9 on bare-metal GPU (air-gapped). Normalization: QuickUMLS 2.6 for SNOMED CT, RxNorm SQLite 2025Q4 snapshot for air-gapped. Metadata: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS. Artifacts: S3. Monitoring: Prometheus + Grafana. CI: GitHub Actions. Package registry: internal PyPI (devpi).
target_output.functional_feature_map: Clinical NER extraction: ClinicalNLPClient.extract_batch(notes, model_version) and extract_single(note, model_version) returning ClinicalConcept objects with entity_type (diagnosis/medication/dosage/procedure), span_start, span_end, normalized_code, ontology (snomed/rxnorm), confidence_score. Model version pinning: client resolves model_version string to artifact S3 key, verifies SHA-256 before loading. Validation testing: run_validation(corpus_id, model_version) computes per-entity-type precision/recall against annotation_sets and writes aggregate row to extracted_concept_logs. Air-gapped bundle: tar archive containing model.pt, tokenizer files, config.json, QuickUMLS index files, and RxNorm SQLite snapshot.
target_output.system_components: ClinicalNLPClient (public entry point), ExtractorPipeline (spaCy + Transformers NER), InferenceBackend (abstract with SageMakerBackend and TorchServeBackend implementations), ConceptNormalizer (QuickUMLS SNOMED CT + RxNorm SQLite/API), ModelRegistry (PostgreSQL version management with SHA-256 verification), ValidationRunner (computes F1 against annotation_sets corpus), PostgreSQL 16 on RDS db.t3.medium, S3 artifact and corpus bucket.
target_output.workflows: Batch Extraction: data scientist calls client.extract_batch(['Note text...'], model_version='clinicalbert-v2.3'). ModelRegistry resolves artifact S3 key, downloads if not cached, verifies SHA-256. ExtractorPipeline tokenizes each note, runs ClinicalBERT NER returning entity spans. ConceptNormalizer maps each span to SNOMED CT or RxNorm code. Results returned as list[ExtractionResult]; notes with inference errors return error_code without raising. Structured log entry records note_id, model_version, entity_count_by_type, latency_ms -- no note text. Model Release Validation: CI runs ValidationRunner against labeled i2b2 corpus with 500 annotated notes. Per-entity-type precision and recall computed; aggregate row written to extracted_concept_logs. GitHub Actions gate blocks PyPI publish if diagnosis_f1 < 0.95 or medication_f1 < 0.95. Passing artifact tagged and published to internal devpi server.
target_output.data_model: model_versions (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR(100), artifact_s3_key TEXT, artifact_sha256 CHAR(64), framework_version VARCHAR, training_corpus VARCHAR, snomed_index_version VARCHAR, rxnorm_snapshot_date DATE, published_at TIMESTAMPTZ, is_active BOOLEAN). annotation_sets (id UUID PK, team_id VARCHAR, corpus_name VARCHAR, note_count INT, label_schema_json JSONB with entity_types array, dua_reference VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). extracted_concept_logs (id UUID PK, job_id UUID, model_version_id UUID FK, note_count INT, entity_counts_json JSONB storing {diagnosis:N, medication:N, dosage:N, procedure:N}, precision_by_type_json JSONB, recall_by_type_json JSONB, corpus_id UUID NULLABLE FK, run_at TIMESTAMPTZ). No column in any table stores clinical note content.
target_output.api_design: SDK public Python API -- ClinicalNLPClient(model_version: str, endpoint_mode: Literal['sagemaker','local']='sagemaker', jwt_token: str). extract_single(note: str) -> ExtractionResult. extract_batch(notes: list[str], concurrency: int=8) -> list[ExtractionResult]. run_validation(corpus_id: str, model_version: str) -> ValidationReport(f1_by_entity_type: dict, precision_by_type: dict, recall_by_type: dict). list_model_versions() -> list[ModelVersionMeta]. ModelRegistry internal REST: GET /model-versions/{id}, POST /validation-runs. No external public REST API -- SDK consumed as Python library only.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HIPAA controls: all note content processed in-memory only, discarded after extraction, never written to disk or logs. Structured logs contain only note_id, entity_count_by_type, model_version, latency_ms. JWT tokens carry team_id and project_scope claims; SageMakerBackend and TorchServeBackend validate claims before invocation. TLS 1.3 for all SageMaker, TorchServe, and PostgreSQL connections. S3 artifact bucket policy restricts GetObject to SDK IAM role; no ListBucket permissions on clinical data prefixes. Dependency pinning with hash verification in pyproject.toml prevents supply chain substitution. HIPAA DUA tracking: annotation_sets.dua_reference links each corpus to institutional agreement; corpus files remain in health-system-controlled storage.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Connected deployment: SageMaker endpoint (ml.g4dn.xlarge, min 1 / max 4 auto-scaling instances) accessed via VPC PrivateLink; no public internet egress path for clinical data processing. Air-gapped deployment: TorchServe 0.9 on hospital-provisioned GPU server (>=16GB VRAM); artifact bundle (model.pt, tokenizer/, config.json, quml_index/, rxnorm.sqlite) transferred via encrypted SFTP within hospital network; SDK reads LOCAL_ENDPOINT_URL env var to route inference to local host. Environments: dev (SageMaker mock via moto, synthetic notes), staging (real SageMaker endpoint, synthetic notes), production (connected or air-gapped per hospital policy). CI/CD: GitHub Actions -- ruff lint, mypy --strict, pytest with synthetic fixtures (zero PHI), moto integration tests, model release F1 gate, devpi publish on pass.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics exported per SDK process: clinical_nlp_extraction_latency_ms histogram (labels: model_version, endpoint_mode, batch_size_bucket), clinical_nlp_concept_count_total counter (labels: entity_type, model_version), clinical_nlp_batch_errors_total counter (labels: error_code), clinical_nlp_f1_by_entity_type gauge (labels: model_version, entity_type -- populated at release from extracted_concept_logs, not per-inference). Grafana dashboard: latency p50/p95/p99 per model version, entity type distribution per team, F1 score history across model versions by entity type (diagnosis, medication, dosage, procedure). PagerDuty alert: error rate >5% in 5-minute sliding window.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: SageMaker ml.g4dn.xlarge at $0.736/hr; 1-4 instance auto-scaling on batch queue depth. Air-gapped path has zero cloud inference cost after hardware provisioning. PostgreSQL RDS db.t3.medium ~$50/month for model registry. S3 artifact storage <10GB for all model bundles. Total connected cost $250-600/month depending on batch volume from data science teams.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (delivered, MVP): ClinicalBERT NER for diagnosis and medication entities, SageMaker connected path, model version pinning, PostgreSQL model_versions table, GitHub Actions CI with synthetic test fixtures. Phase 2 (this round): TorchServe air-gapped path with documented artifact bundle format, SHA-256 checksum verification, dosage and procedure entity types added, extracted_concept_logs with per-entity-type F1 columns, F1 >= 0.95 CI release gate, clinical_nlp_f1_by_entity_type Prometheus gauge. Phase 3 (Year 2): Streaming inference for live note ingestion via Kafka consumer, relation extraction linking medication mentions to diagnosis codes, annotation UI for internal corpus labeling, Spanish clinical note support via multilingual ClinicalBERT variant.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with pyproject.toml and hatch build backend. mypy --strict enforced in CI; no type: ignore comments without issue reference. ruff for linting and formatting. pytest with synthetic note fixtures only -- real patient data never used in tests. All public API return types defined as frozen dataclasses with complete field documentation. Semantic versioning: SDK semver and model versions tracked independently. Breaking changes to ExtractionResult fields require major version bump with migration guide.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ClinicalBERT performance degrades on notes from specialties underrepresented in MIMIC-III training data (pediatrics, ophthalmology, psychiatry). Mitigation: per-entity-type F1 tracked in extracted_concept_logs enables specialty-specific degradation detection; specialty-tuned models planned for Year 2. Risk: QuickUMLS SNOMED CT index staleness as SNOMED releases twice yearly. Mitigation: snomed_index_version tracked in model_versions table; index update pipeline added to Year 2 roadmap. Tradeoff: local SQLite RxNorm snapshot adds 200MB to air-gapped artifact bundle but eliminates all network dependency for air-gapped hospitals. Tradeoff: aggregate-only metrics in extracted_concept_logs prevent per-note accuracy analysis but are required to avoid PHI persistence.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: TorchServe 0.9 selected over Triton Inference Server for air-gapped path due to simpler Python-native model configuration for ClinicalBERT. SHA-256 checksums stored in model_versions.artifact_sha256, verified on every artifact load. extracted_concept_logs precision_by_type_json and recall_by_type_json columns close ISS-003. CI F1 gate (>=0.95 diagnosis and medication) prevents publishing degraded model releases.

project_goal.rationale: Core need for integration engines processing thousands of HL7 messages per second from hospital information systems

target_users.rationale: Primary consumers are engineers building HIS/LIS/RIS integration layers

project_class.rationale: Distributed as a Go module, not a hosted service

capabilities.rationale: High-performance parsing, message routing, and HL7 segment transformation with no UI layer

complexity_level.rationale: Sub-millisecond parse targets require zero-allocation design; HL7 v2 segment grammar is complex with conditional fields and delimiter escaping

risk_level.rationale: Library processes PHI in HL7 messages but operates in-process; no network exposure of its own

backend_stack.rationale: Zero-dependency core maximizes embeddability; Go chosen for sub-millisecond parse performance

project.goal: Zero-dependency Go library for sub-millisecond HL7 v2 parsing and routing
project.target_users: Integration engineers building hospital HIS/LIS/RIS middleware
project.timeline: v1.0 for Q3 integration engine adoption

backend.framework: No framework; pure Go standard library for core parser
backend.api: Go package API with typed structs for each message type and fluent builder for outbound construction

security.auth: N/A -- library is in-process; host engine owns auth boundary
security.transport: N/A -- library does not own transport layer; sanitize_phi() helper for safe logging
security.phi_handling: Library logs zero PHI; error records sanitized before optional PostgreSQL write

devops.hosting: Go module on internal Gitea / pkg.go.dev
devops.environments: No runtime environments; test matrix for linux/amd64 and linux/arm64

constraints.dependencies: Zero external modules in core parser

open_questions.fhir_adapter: FHIR R4 transformation deferred to Year 2

confirmed_decisions.architecture: Zero-dependency core with optional integration modules

reasoner_reviews.product: The zero-dependency constraint is architecturally sound and essential for embeddability in diverse integration engine environments. ADT and ORU message types covering admission/discharge and lab results represent the highest-volume HL7 traffic in hospital environments, making the MVP scope well-targeted. The fluent builder API for outbound message construction is a high-value feature -- integration engineers currently manage delimiter escaping manually, which is a common source of malformed message bugs.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Achieving sub-0.5ms parse times requires a zero-allocation parser for the hot path. Go's slice-based HL7 segment splitting with pre-allocated buffers is the right approach -- avoid reflection-based field mapping which introduces significant overhead. The routing_rules engine should use a compiled rule set loaded at startup rather than evaluating rules on every message. The optional PostgreSQL module for persistent routing rules needs connection pooling guidance for integration engines handling high message volumes.
reasoner_reviews.security: The library must enforce a strict no-PHI logging contract. The sanitize_phi() helper is a good pattern but must be well-documented since integration engineers may not know which HL7 fields contain PHI. Consider providing a SafeLogMessage type that wraps a parsed message and exposes only non-PHI fields (message_type, control_id, timestamp, segment_count) for structured logging. HIPAA transmission security is the host engine's responsibility but the library readme must make this explicit.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The zero-external-dependency constraint is feasible for the core parser but the optional PostgreSQL module will require pgx or lib/pq. Document this clearly -- the optional module has external dependencies; only the core go-hl7 module is zero-dep. The linux/arm64 build target requires testing on actual ARM hardware or QEMU in CI to catch endianness assumptions in byte-slice operations.

backend.service_design: Package structure: github.com/org/go-hl7 (core parser, zero deps), github.com/org/go-hl7/routing (rule-based dispatch, zero deps), github.com/org/go-hl7/builder (outbound message construction, zero deps), github.com/org/go-hl7/storage (PostgreSQL routing_rules and parse_errors, depends on pgx/v5), github.com/org/go-hl7/metrics (Prometheus metrics, depends on prometheus/client_golang).
backend.api_patterns: Core parser API: Parse(raw []byte) (*HL7Message, error). HL7Message struct with MessageType (ADT/ORM/ORU/MDM), EventType (A01/A08/R01), Segments map[string][]Segment, ControlID string. Segment struct with FieldValues []string and GetField(index int) string accessor. Router: NewRouter().Handle('ADT_A01', handlerFunc).Handle('ORU_R01', handlerFunc). Dispatch(msg *HL7Message) error. Builder: NewADT_A01Builder().SetPatientID(mrn).SetPatientName(last, first).SetAdmitDateTime(t).Build() returns []byte HL7 message.
backend.failure_handling: Parse errors return typed ParseError with ErrorType (malformed_segment/missing_required_field/invalid_delimiter/unknown_message_type), SegmentName, FieldIndex, and RawFragment (sanitized, no PHI). Router dispatch errors are non-blocking -- failed handlers log error and continue to next registered handler for the message type. Malformed ACKs log warning and return AA (accept) to avoid blocking sender queues.
backend.scaling_notes: Zero-allocation hot path: Parse() uses a single pre-allocated byte slice pool per goroutine via sync.Pool. Benchmark target: BenchmarkParseADT_A01 < 500 microseconds at 1000 iterations with zero heap allocations on the parse path. Router uses a pre-compiled jump table keyed by message_type+event_type for O(1) handler lookup.
backend.backend_risks: HL7 v2 segment grammar variations across EHR vendors (Epic, Cerner, Meditech) may produce non-standard delimiter sequences or Z-segments not covered by the spec. Mitigation: permissive parsing mode that accepts non-standard delimiters with a warning; Z-segment passthrough to handler as raw bytes.

security.auth_design: No authentication in library -- in-process module embedded by host engine. Host engine is responsible for authenticating HL7 message sources at the MLLP or TCP layer.
security.privacy_controls: sanitize_phi() function accepts *HL7Message and returns SafeLogMessage with only MessageType, EventType, ControlID, SendingApplication, ReceivingApplication, MessageTimestamp. All PHI-bearing segments (PID, PV1, OBX, ORC, NTE) excluded from SafeLogMessage. parse_errors storage module writes ErrorType, SegmentName, FieldIndex only -- never the field value that caused the error.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Library itself emits only structured log lines containing SafeLogMessage fields. Integration engineers instructed via documentation to pass structured logs through the host engine audit pipeline. Library readme includes HIPAA compliance note explaining PHI handling responsibility boundaries.
security.security_risks: Integration engineers may bypass sanitize_phi() and log raw HL7Message structs in development then forget to revert in production. Mitigation: HL7Message.String() method deliberately omits PHI fields (returns SafeLogMessage JSON) so fmt.Printf logging of the struct is automatically safe.

data.entities: routing_rules (id UUID PK, message_type VARCHAR, event_type VARCHAR, condition_expression TEXT, handler_name VARCHAR, priority INT, is_active BOOLEAN, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). parse_errors (id UUID PK, message_type VARCHAR, event_type NULLABLE, error_type VARCHAR, segment_name VARCHAR, field_index INT, sending_application VARCHAR, occurred_at TIMESTAMPTZ). No PHI stored in any table.
data.schema_notes: routing_rules.condition_expression stores a simple boolean expression evaluated against SafeLogMessage fields (e.g., 'sending_application == EPIC_ADT'). Core parser loads rules at startup into compiled rule set; live reload via SIGHUP for zero-downtime rule updates. parse_errors expires after 30 days via PostgreSQL partitioning by month.
data.data_risks: condition_expression column could theoretically reference PHI fields from a message; library evaluates expressions only against SafeLogMessage non-PHI fields. Integration engineers attempting PHI-based routing must use custom handler code, not routing_rules expressions.

devops.deployment_topology: Library distributed as Go module; no runtime infrastructure. Optional storage module requires PostgreSQL 16 accessible from the host integration engine pod. Bare-metal Kubernetes integration engine pods mount library as compiled binary dependency.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI pipeline: go vet, staticcheck, golangci-lint; go test -race ./... on linux/amd64 and linux/arm64 (QEMU); go test -bench=./... with performance regression gate (BenchmarkParseADT_A01 must not regress >5% vs baseline); go build -tags integration for optional modules. Module release: tag v1.x.x triggers pkg.go.dev index update and internal Gitea mirror.
devops.observability_stack: Optional metrics module exposes Prometheus counters and histograms: hl7_parse_total (labels: message_type, event_type, status), hl7_parse_duration_microseconds histogram (labels: message_type), hl7_route_dispatch_total (labels: message_type, handler_name, status), hl7_parse_errors_total (labels: error_type, message_type). Grafana dashboard template shipped in repository /dashboards directory.
devops.devops_risks: CI QEMU arm64 emulation adds 3-4x test execution time. Mitigation: arm64 tests run only on release branches, not every commit. Go module proxy caching on internal Gitea prevents breakage if pkg.go.dev is unavailable in air-gapped hospital environments.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a zero-dependency Go library for sub-millisecond HL7 v2 parsing. Key decisions: zero-allocation hot path via sync.Pool byte buffers for parse performance; package split between zero-dep core and optional pgx/Prometheus modules for embeddability; SafeLogMessage type enforcing PHI-safe logging without requiring engineers to remember which HL7 fields are PHI. The library itself has no network exposure so HIPAA controls focus on preventing PHI from appearing in error logs and metrics.
target_output.title: go-hl7: High-Throughput HL7 v2 Parser and Router Library
target_output.executive_summary: A zero-external-dependency Go 1.22 library for parsing, validating, routing, and constructing HL7 v2 messages at sub-millisecond latency. Supports ADT (A01/A08/A03), ORU (R01), ORM (O01), and MDM (T02) message types with a zero-allocation parse path, rule-based handler dispatch, fluent outbound builder API, and built-in PHI-safe logging utilities. Optional integration modules add PostgreSQL-backed routing rule persistence and Prometheus metrics emission.
target_output.architecture_overview: Modular Go package structure with a zero-external-dependency core (go-hl7 parse, routing, builder sub-packages) and optional integration modules (storage with pgx/v5, metrics with prometheus/client_golang). Core parser uses a single-pass byte slice scanner with pre-allocated segment buffers pooled via sync.Pool to achieve zero heap allocations on the hot parse path. Handler dispatch uses a pre-compiled jump table keyed by message_type+event_type for O(1) lookup. HL7Message.String() deliberately returns SafeLogMessage JSON so accidental fmt.Printf logging of parsed messages never exposes PHI.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.22 with generics for typed segment accessors. Core modules: zero external dependencies (standard library only). Storage module: pgx/v5 for PostgreSQL routing_rules and parse_errors. Metrics module: prometheus/client_golang client. Build: go build with separate build tags (core, integration, metrics). CI: GitLab CI with linux/amd64 and linux/arm64 (QEMU) test matrices. Benchmarking: go test -bench with 5% regression gate. Distribution: Go module on internal Gitea and pkg.go.dev.
target_output.functional_feature_map: ADT parsing: A01 (admit), A08 (update), A03 (discharge) with PID, PV1, EVN segment extraction into PatientAdmissionEvent struct. ORU parsing: R01 lab result messages with OBR, OBX segment extraction into LabResultEvent with ObservationResult slice. Outbound builder: NewADT_A01Builder() fluent API setting PID, PV1 fields, generating properly escaped HL7 v2.5 byte output with correct segment terminators. Routing: NewRouter().Handle(msgType_eventType, HandlerFunc) dispatching parsed messages to registered handlers. ACK generation: GenerateACK(msg, code) returning AA/AE/AR acknowledgment messages. PHI-safe logging: sanitize_phi(msg) -> SafeLogMessage with control metadata only.
target_output.system_components: Parser (core/parser.go): single-pass byte scanner, segment splitter, field extractor, zero-allocation hot path with sync.Pool. MessageTypeRegistry (core/registry.go): maps MSH-9 components to typed message structs. Router (routing/router.go): pre-compiled jump table, handler registration, dispatch with error isolation. Builder (builder/): fluent constructors for ADT and ORU message types with delimiter escaping. SafeLogMessage (core/safe_log.go): PHI-stripped log-safe message representation. Storage module (storage/): pgx/v5-backed routing_rules and parse_errors CRUD. Metrics module (metrics/): Prometheus counter and histogram registration and emission.
target_output.workflows: Inbound ADT Parsing: Integration engine receives raw HL7 bytes from MLLP socket. Calls hl7.Parse(rawBytes) returning *HL7Message or ParseError. Router.Dispatch(msg) looks up ADT_A01 handler in jump table, invokes handler with typed PatientAdmissionEvent. Handler processes admission event (e.g., creates encounter in EHR database). Router generates ACK via hl7.GenerateACK(msg, 'AA') returned to sender. Structured log emits SafeLogMessage JSON with MessageType, ControlID, SendingApplication only. Outbound Lab Result Construction: LIS system calls hl7.NewORU_R01Builder().SetPatientID(mrn).SetObservation(testCode, value, units, abnormalFlag).SetOrderingProvider(npi).Build(). Builder produces properly escaped ORU^R01 byte slice with correct OBX segment ordering. Integration engine writes bytes to MLLP socket to receiving system.
target_output.data_model: routing_rules (id UUID PK, message_type VARCHAR(10) e.g. 'ADT', event_type VARCHAR(10) e.g. 'A01', condition_expression TEXT evaluated against SafeLogMessage fields only, handler_name VARCHAR(100), priority INT DEFAULT 100, is_active BOOLEAN DEFAULT true, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). parse_errors (id UUID PK, message_type VARCHAR(10), event_type VARCHAR(10) NULLABLE, error_type VARCHAR(50) ENUM malformed_segment/missing_required_field/invalid_delimiter/unknown_message_type, segment_name VARCHAR(10), field_index INT, sending_application VARCHAR(50), occurred_at TIMESTAMPTZ). Partitioned by occurred_at monthly; partitions dropped after 30 days. No PHI in any column. Clinical message entities: patient_demographics (msg_id FK, patient_mrn_hash VARCHAR, date_of_birth_year INT, gender_code CHAR(1), insurance_plan_id VARCHAR), encounter_events (id UUID PK, msg_id FK, encounter_id VARCHAR, event_type ENUM admission/discharge/transfer, attending_physician_npi VARCHAR, facility_oid VARCHAR, admit_diagnosis_icd10 VARCHAR), hl7_ack_log (id UUID PK, msg_id FK, ack_code ENUM AA/AE/AR, ack_timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ, error_detail TEXT).
target_output.api_design: Go package API -- Parse(raw []byte) (*HL7Message, error). GetSegment(name string) (Segment, bool). GetField(segment string, index int) string. Router.Handle(pattern string, fn HandlerFunc) *Router. Router.Dispatch(msg *HL7Message) error. GenerateACK(msg *HL7Message, code AckCode) []byte. sanitize_phi(msg *HL7Message) SafeLogMessage. Fluent builders: NewADT_A01Builder() MessageBuilder with SetPatientID(mrn), SetPatientName(last, first), SetAdmitDateTime(time.Time), SetAttendingPhysician(npi), Build() []byte. Error types: ParseError{Type, Segment, FieldIndex, RawFragment} -- RawFragment truncated to 20 bytes with PHI fields zeroed.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HIPAA transmission security: library has no transport layer; host integration engine must use TLS 1.3 or MLLP over TLS for HL7 message transmission. PHI-safe logging: HL7Message.String() returns SafeLogMessage JSON including only MessageType, EventType, ControlID, SendingApplication, ReceivingApplication, Timestamp -- all PID, PV1, OBX segment content excluded. ParseError.RawFragment truncated and PHI field values zeroed before any logging. parse_errors PostgreSQL table stores only structural error metadata, never field values. Library readme includes explicit HIPAA compliance notes explaining host engine responsibilities for transport security and audit logging.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Library deployment: go get github.com/org/go-hl7@v1.0.0 in integration engine go.mod. No runtime infrastructure for core library. Optional storage module requires PostgreSQL 16 on same Kubernetes namespace as integration engine pod; connection pool via pgx/v5 pgxpool with max 10 connections per engine pod. Bare-metal Kubernetes pods running integration engines mount library as compiled Go binary dependency. GitLab CI pipeline: go vet, staticcheck, golangci-lint, go test -race (amd64 + arm64 QEMU), BenchmarkParseADT_A01 regression gate (<=500 microseconds, <=5% degradation vs baseline). Release: semver tag triggers Gitea mirror and pkg.go.dev index. Breaking changes require major version bump with migration guide.
target_output.observability: Optional metrics module registers Prometheus metrics: hl7_parse_total counter (labels: message_type, event_type, status=success/error), hl7_parse_duration_microseconds histogram with buckets [100, 250, 500, 1000, 2000, 5000] (labels: message_type), hl7_route_dispatch_total counter (labels: message_type, event_type, handler_name, status), hl7_parse_errors_total counter (labels: error_type, message_type). Grafana dashboard template in /dashboards/go-hl7-overview.json shows parse latency p95 by message type, error rate heatmap by error_type, dispatch throughput by handler. Parse benchmarks tracked in CI as performance observability.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Library has zero infrastructure cost for core parsing -- in-process Go module. Optional PostgreSQL storage module adds RDS t3.micro ($15/month) for routing rules if centralized persistence needed; most integration engines load rules from config files at startup. Message throughput scales linearly with integration engine goroutine pool size; benchmark shows 40,000 ADT_A01 parses/second per CPU core on commodity x86-64.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 / v1.0 (8 weeks): ADT A01/A08/A03 and ORU R01 parsing, Router with jump-table dispatch, SafeLogMessage PHI-safe logging, fluent ADT builder, ACK generation, zero-allocation benchmark suite, GitLab CI with arm64 QEMU tests, PostgreSQL routing_rules and parse_errors optional module, Prometheus metrics optional module. Phase 2 / v1.1 (4 weeks): ORM O01 pharmacy order parsing, MDM T02 medical document notification parsing, ORU R01 builder, MLLP framing utilities for integration engines handling raw TCP sockets. Phase 3 / v2.0 (Year 2): HL7 v2 to FHIR R4 transformation adapter, Kafka consumer module for batch HL7 stream processing, streaming parser for large multi-message batches.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go 1.22 with go.mod sum verification. Zero external dependencies for core, storage, and metrics modules tracked separately with explicit upgrade policy. golangci-lint with errcheck, staticcheck, govet enabled. go test -race on all packages. Benchmark regression gate: BenchmarkParseADT_A01 must stay <=500 microseconds mean; tracked in CI artifact per commit. All exported types documented with godoc comments including PHI handling notes. Breaking API changes require major version bump; deprecation notice in one minor release before removal. HL7 v2 vendor compatibility: test suite includes real (de-identified) message samples from Epic, Cerner, and Meditech.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: HL7 v2 vendor-specific extensions (Z-segments, non-standard delimiters) from Epic or Meditech break strict parsers. Mitigation: permissive parsing mode passes unknown Z-segments as raw bytes to handler; delimiter autodetection from MSH-1 and MSH-2 instead of assuming pipe/caret. Risk: sync.Pool buffer reuse causes data races if integration engineers hold references to Segment.FieldValues slices after handler returns. Mitigation: Segment.GetField(index) returns string copies, not slice references; document slice aliasing hazard prominently in godoc. Tradeoff: zero-dependency constraint prevents using well-tested HL7 parsing libraries (e.g., hapi-hl7v2 equivalent) but is required for broad embeddability in hospital environments with strict dependency policies.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed: zero-dependency core with optional module split for PostgreSQL and Prometheus, keeping core embeddable without transitive dependencies. Confirmed: HL7Message.String() returns SafeLogMessage by default to prevent accidental PHI exposure via fmt logging. Selected Go 1.22 generics for typed segment accessors over interface{} to improve IDE support and reduce runtime type assertion overhead.

metadata.notes: Go HL7 v2 library with zero-allocation parse path, PHI-safe logging utilities, and optional PostgreSQL/Prometheus modules.

profile.domain: Readmission Risk Scoring SDK for EHR Frontends

project_goal.value: JavaScript/TypeScript SDK for integrating predictive readmission risk scoring into hospital EHR front-end applications, surfacing risk scores, contributing features, and intervention recommendations in a UI-embeddable widget format
project_goal.rationale: Clinicians need readmission risk scores surfaced in-context within the EHR workflow to drive care management decisions

target_users.value: EHR front-end engineers integrating risk widgets into clinical workflows; clinicians consuming risk scores and recommendations within EHR screens
target_users.rationale: SDK consumers are engineers; end users are bedside clinicians

project_class.rationale: Distributed as npm package embedded in EHR front-end applications; no standalone hosting

capabilities.rationale: REST calls to risk scoring endpoint, score caching, EHR task integration, ML model metadata management

complexity_level.rationale: SDK wraps a model-serving API with caching and EHR integration hooks; moderate complexity with clear boundaries

risk_level.rationale: Risk scores influence clinical decisions; incorrect scores or stale cache could contribute to patient harm; FDA SaMD guidance may apply

data_sensitivity.rationale: SDK sends patient encounter context to risk scoring endpoint containing PHI; scores are PHI if linked to a patient

external_exposure.rationale: SDK embedded in internal EHR applications; risk scoring endpoint on internal Azure network

access_model.value: Azure Active Directory tokens passed through from EHR session; SDK does not manage its own auth; tokens scoped to risk-scoring-api audience
access_model.rationale: Inheriting EHR session tokens avoids separate credential management for each clinician

feature_scope.value: Risk score fetch with session caching; contributing feature panel (top SHAP features); intervention recommendation display; clinician alert acknowledgement with EHR task creation; model metadata display (version, training date, AUC)
feature_scope.rationale: Full workflow from score display to care management action

mvp_scope.value: Risk score badge with expandable contributing features panel; session caching; AAD token passthrough; no care management task creation in MVP
mvp_scope.rationale: Core display and caching needed for clinical pilot; task integration deferred to v1.1

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 for all calls to risk scoring endpoint; AAD token validation on every request; no risk scores persisted to localStorage or sessionStorage; cache in-memory only
security_baseline.rationale: HIPAA requires PHI not stored client-side; in-memory session cache scoped to browser tab

frontend_stack.value: N/A - library_sdk distributed as npm package; consumer EHR apps may use React, Angular, or Vue
frontend_stack.rationale: SDK is framework-agnostic TypeScript; widget components published separately as optional React package

backend_stack.value: Node.js 22 with TypeScript; risk scoring model served on Azure Container Apps (separate service owned by ML team)
backend_stack.rationale: TypeScript SDK for type safety and EHR frontend compatibility; model serving owned by ML team

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for score_history and model_metadata on Azure Database for PostgreSQL; in-memory cache for session scores (no Redis for SDK, host app may add Redis)
data_platform.rationale: Score history enables audit trail and score trend display; in-memory cache avoids PHI in shared cache stores

hosting_target.value: npm package published to internal Azure Artifacts registry; risk scoring endpoint on Azure Container Apps in internal VNet
hosting_target.rationale: Controlled distribution via internal registry; VNet isolation for risk endpoint

privacy_retention_policy.value: Score history retained 2 years for clinical audit; in-memory session cache cleared on page unload; no PHI in browser storage
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: HIPAA audit trail requirement for clinical decision support tools

future_scope.value: Care management EHR task creation on alert acknowledgement; multi-model support (sepsis risk, fall risk); proactive push notifications for deteriorating score trends

constraints.value: SDK bundle size must not exceed 80KB gzipped to avoid degrading EHR app load time; must work in EHR browser sandbox environments (no eval(), no dynamic import of external URLs)
constraints.rationale: EHR security sandboxes restrict dynamic code execution; bundle size impacts clinical workflow load times

observability_baseline.value: Datadog RUM integration for widget render times and API latency; structured log events for score fetch, cache hit/miss, and acknowledgement actions sent to host EHR logging pipeline
observability_baseline.rationale: Clinical performance monitoring required for SaMD audit trail and EHR vendor SLAs

execution_preference.value: Two-week sprints; npm semver with patch/minor releases; no breaking changes without major version bump and migration guide
execution_preference.rationale: EHR integration teams need stable versioning for compliance-tested deployments

compliance_context.value: HIPAA for PHI in patient encounter context sent to risk endpoint; FDA Software as a Medical Device guidance if risk scores directly influence clinical treatment decisions; institutional IRB approval for model training data
compliance_context.rationale: Clinical decision support tools in EHRs may be subject to SaMD classification depending on intended use claims

llm_integration.value: Gradient boosted tree model (XGBoost) served by ML team on Azure Container Apps endpoint; SDK calls POST /v1/risk-scores with encounter_id and feature_vector; response includes score (0-1), risk_tier (low/medium/high), top_features (SHAP values), intervention_recommendations, model_version, model_auc
llm_integration.rationale: XGBoost model trained on 3 years of EHR encounter data; SHAP values enable clinician-interpretable contributing factors display

project.goal: TypeScript SDK for embeddable readmission risk score widget in EHR frontends
project.target_users: EHR frontend engineers and bedside clinicians

backend.framework: TypeScript with tsc; no runtime framework dependency
backend.api: Internal npm API: RiskScoringClient with getRiskScore(), clearCache(), acknowledgeAlert()

security.auth: AAD token passthrough from EHR session
security.phi_handling: No PHI in browser storage; in-memory session cache only

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for score_history and model_metadata
data.cache: In-memory Map per session; no Redis

devops.environments: dev, staging, production risk endpoint environments

open_questions.task_creation: EHR care management task creation deferred to v1.1

reasoner_reviews.product: The in-memory session cache design correctly handles HIPAA PHI constraints -- risk scores must not persist in localStorage or sessionStorage where other scripts could access them. The widget architecture being framework-agnostic TypeScript core with an optional React component package is the right approach given the heterogeneous EHR technology landscape (Epic Hyperspace uses React, some older EHRs use Angular). The 80KB bundle constraint is tight but achievable given the SDK only wraps HTTP calls and maintains a cache.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The previous round plan had an underspecified cache invalidation strategy. Session-scoped in-memory cache using a Map keyed by encounter_id is correct, but the plan must specify when stale scores are evicted -- scores should expire after a configurable TTL (default 15 minutes) and must be invalidated when encounter_id changes to a different patient. Round 2 also left the score_history PostgreSQL schema incomplete -- missing model_version and acknowledgement_at columns needed for SaMD audit requirements.
reasoner_reviews.security: AAD token passthrough is sound but the SDK must validate that the token is still valid before each risk score request -- expired tokens passed from the EHR session could cause silent 401 failures without informing the clinician. The score_history table must be append-only (no UPDATE or DELETE) to satisfy SaMD audit trail requirements. The SDK should emit a structured audit event for every score display, including score, risk_tier, model_version, and clinician_id.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 80KB gzip constraint is achievable if the SDK avoids bundling polyfills and relies on the EHR app's ES2020+ target. Tree-shaking in esbuild will eliminate unused widget components. The optional React widget package should be a separate npm package to prevent adding React as a mandatory SDK dependency for non-React EHR apps.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The contributing features panel was specified in the feature scope but its rendering contract was undefined in round 2. The SDK must specify the TopFeature interface (feature_name, shap_value, direction: positive/negative, clinical_description) and the maximum number of features to display (top 5 by absolute SHAP value). Clinicians need clinical_description strings from the model endpoint, not raw feature names like 'prior_admission_count_90d'.

backend.service_design: SDK package structure: @org/risk-scoring-sdk (core TypeScript, zero runtime framework dependencies). Exports: RiskScoringClient class, RiskScore interface, TopFeature interface, RiskTier enum, AuditEvent interface. Optional: @org/risk-scoring-widget (React 18 components using core SDK). RiskScoringClient constructor accepts RiskScoringConfig {endpointUrl, aadTokenProvider: () => Promise<string>, cacheTTLSeconds: number, onAuditEvent: (event: AuditEvent) => void}.
backend.api_patterns: getRiskScore(encounterId: string, featureVector: EncounterFeatures): Promise<RiskScore>. RiskScore {score: number, riskTier: RiskTier, topFeatures: TopFeature[], interventionRecommendations: string[], modelVersion: string, modelAuc: number, scoredAt: Date, fromCache: boolean}. clearCache(encounterId?: string): void. acknowledgeAlert(encounterId: string, action: AcknowledgementAction): Promise<void>. All methods emit AuditEvent via onAuditEvent callback.
backend.llm_integration_design: SDK calls POST /v1/risk-scores on ML team's Azure Container Apps endpoint. Request body: {encounter_id, feature_vector: EncounterFeatures}. AAD token fetched via aadTokenProvider() before each uncached request; token cached for its validity period. Response mapped to RiskScore interface. topFeatures sorted by Math.abs(shap_value) descending; top 5 returned. Model version and AUC stored in score_history for audit.
backend.failure_handling: Network errors return RiskScoreError with errorCode (network_error/auth_error/model_unavailable/timeout) and display_message safe for clinician display. Stale cached scores returned with fromCache=true and cache_age_seconds on cache hit. If model endpoint returns 503, SDK returns last valid cached score if within 60 minutes, otherwise surfaces RiskScoreError to EHR app for graceful degradation display.

security.auth_design: AAD token fetched via injected aadTokenProvider() before each uncached API call. SDK checks token expiry (JWT exp claim) before request; if expires within 60 seconds, calls aadTokenProvider() for fresh token. No tokens stored in SDK state beyond request lifetime. score_history writes use service account credentials from Azure Key Vault via environment variable, not the clinician AAD token.
security.privacy_controls: No PHI stored in browser localStorage, sessionStorage, or IndexedDB. In-memory cache uses Map<encounterId, {score: RiskScore, expiresAt: Date}> scoped to RiskScoringClient instance (tab-scoped). Cache cleared on client.clearCache() or page unload event. Structured audit events contain encounterId, clinician_id (from AAD token sub claim), action, model_version, score, risk_tier -- no patient name or demographics.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: AuditEvent emitted on: score_displayed, cache_hit, alert_acknowledged, score_fetch_error. AuditEvent {eventType, encounterId, clinicianId, score, riskTier, modelVersion, timestamp}. Host EHR application is responsible for persisting AuditEvents to its audit pipeline; SDK provides the data. score_history PostgreSQL table written by SDK backend sidecar (not browser-side) on acknowledgeAlert() calls via secure server-side endpoint.
security.security_risks: EHR apps passing expired AAD tokens could silently serve stale cached scores beyond TTL. Mitigation: explicit token expiry check before each uncached call. bundle_size constraint means no JWT decode library -- use atob() to decode base64 exp claim manually.

data.entities: risk_scores (id UUID PK, encounter_id VARCHAR, patient_id VARCHAR, score NUMERIC(5,4), risk_tier VARCHAR CHECK IN ('low','medium','high'), top_features_json JSONB, intervention_recommendations_json JSONB, model_version VARCHAR, model_auc NUMERIC(5,4), scored_at TIMESTAMPTZ, clinician_id VARCHAR, acknowledged_at TIMESTAMPTZ NULLABLE, acknowledgement_action VARCHAR NULLABLE). model_metadata (id UUID PK, model_version VARCHAR UNIQUE, training_date DATE, auc NUMERIC(5,4), feature_count INT, training_cohort_description TEXT, deployed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, deprecated_at TIMESTAMPTZ NULLABLE).
data.schema_notes: risk_scores is append-only (no UPDATE/DELETE) to satisfy SaMD audit trail requirements; acknowledgement captured via acknowledged_at and acknowledgement_action columns added on acknowledge event. top_features_json stores [{feature_name, shap_value, direction, clinical_description}] for audit and trend analysis without re-querying model endpoint.
data.retention_and_deletion: risk_scores retained 2 years per HIPAA clinical record retention. model_metadata retained indefinitely for audit traceability of which model version scored which encounters. No PHI beyond encounter_id and patient_id stored in risk_scores -- patient demographics not persisted.

devops.deployment_topology: SDK published to internal Azure Artifacts npm registry. Risk scoring endpoint deployed by ML team on Azure Container Apps in internal VNet (not managed by SDK team). PostgreSQL on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server (GP_Standard_D2s_v3, zone-redundant). score_history writes triggered by SDK via secure server-side acknowledgeAlert endpoint, not browser-direct.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: tsc --noEmit type check, eslint, vitest unit tests with mock risk endpoint, esbuild bundle with --bundle --minify; bundle size check gate (gzip output must be <=80KB); npm publish to Azure Artifacts on semver tag. Separate workflow for @org/risk-scoring-widget React package.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog RUM integration: custom action events risk_score_displayed, cache_hit, alert_acknowledged with encounter_id (hashed), risk_tier, model_version, latency_ms. SDK emits timing marks via Performance.mark() for getRiskScore() call start/end. Grafana + Azure Monitor for risk scoring endpoint latency and error rates (ML team managed). Alert: getRiskScore() p95 latency > 2s triggers Datadog monitor.
devops.devops_risks: Azure Artifacts npm registry availability could block EHR app CI pipelines if unavailable. Mitigation: lock SDK version in EHR app package.json; npm ci in CI uses lockfile. esbuild tree-shaking may not eliminate all unused TopFeature rendering code if exports are not properly named -- validate bundle contents with --analyze flag.

ISS-001.title: Cache invalidation strategy undefined -- stale scores for different patients
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan used encounter_id as cache key but did not specify TTL or cross-patient invalidation. If a clinician views patient A then navigates to patient B with a different encounter_id, patient A score could remain in cache if encounter_ids are reused across patients.

ISS-002.title: score_history schema missing model_version and acknowledged_at for SaMD audit
ISS-002.detail: Round 2 score_history table lacked model_version and acknowledged_at columns. SaMD audit trail requires knowing which model version produced a score and whether the clinician acknowledged the alert.

ISS-003.title: TopFeature interface and contributing features rendering contract undefined
ISS-003.detail: Feature scope includes contributing features panel but round 2 plan did not define TopFeature interface or specify how raw SHAP feature names are mapped to clinician-readable descriptions. Displaying 'prior_admission_count_90d' raw to clinicians is a usability failure.

focus_issues.title: TopFeature interface and contributing features rendering contract undefined
focus_issues.detail: Feature scope includes contributing features panel but plan does not define TopFeature interface or how raw SHAP feature names map to clinician-readable descriptions.
